item.title: ❓ About My Questions
item.description: The important sets of questions I ask, introspective and practical: the questions I ask myself to shape who I am, and the ones worth asking before starting something.
item.content:encoded:
This is where I keep the sets of questions I ask. Some are introspective: the questions I ask myself to understand who I am and what I want. Some are practical: the questions worth sitting with before starting a business, a blog, or anything that matters. They are important enough to be their own collection, distinct from the ideas that merely occurred to me and the inputs I keep to shape my thinking.

A question is a flashlight​

A good question does not answer anything for you; it points at the part of the room you have been avoiding. I keep these grouped by what they are really about, revisit a set when its season comes around, and write the answer down so I can watch it move over the years. The practice and the legend behind the introspective sets live in What I Ask Myself.

Two kinds of question set​

  • Introspective: questions I ask myself, to shape who I am. About purpose, fear, worth, inaction, what I want. The "What I Ask Myself" series.
  • Practical: questions worth asking before doing something. Starting a business, starting a blog, deciding what to learn. The "Questions for…" series.

Browse the sets below.

item.title: Questions for Starting a Business
item.description: The questions to work through when starting a business, from business model and audience to legal structure, plus the mindset to keep going.
item.content:encoded:
Starting a business is partly a checklist of questions and partly a mindset. The questions force you to get specific about what you are offering, who it is for, and how it reaches them. The mindset is what keeps you moving when the questions get hard. Here is both.

The dimensions to think through​

A business has many moving parts, and the ones you ignore early are the ones that bite you later. Naming every dimension up front (model, mission, branding, fulfillment, marketing, audience, legal, tax, experience) keeps you from building something with a hole in it.

Why start a business?​

I want to start a business mostly to know what it takes. These questions surface the scope of what you are signing up for (the kinds of businesses that exist, and everything you will have to worry about) before you are in too deep to back out.

What kind of business?​

The shape of the business should fit what you like doing and where you can add value. These questions narrow a huge space (small business or not, what it solves, what you enjoy) down to something you would actually want to run, like the painting-coffee-cups idea I keep coming back to.

A few framing thoughts on how businesses actually work:

  • Businesses consider requests for proof of the value you add.
  • Great magic makes you careful about what you believe in.
  • Business and sales are like a magic trick. It is all about perception.
  • Should product management and engineers have a tense relationship? Why?

Some concrete next steps if you go this route:

  • Figure out your personal products.
  • Figure out what people are most likely to spend their resources on.
  • Figure out how to be more resourceful.
  • Read on: convincing, making a product, starting a business, pleasing others, and behavior.

One pattern worth noticing: people make money off making something easy to use. Tools like Sheetsu and Microsoft Flow are simple examples of turning friction into a product.

What I want to contribute​

Before the mechanics, it helps to name what I actually want to give. For me it is pushing people to rise, learning what it means to create a business, and growing myself in the process. The lists below are my own answers, kept as a record of why I am doing this and what I will need.

What do I want to do?

  • Push people to rise, and gauge where they are motivationally and passionately.
  • Find out what it means to create a business: how a product is built, what relationships need to be made, and the processes involved.
  • Give back to people and push them to be great.

Why do I want to do it?

  • To help others and to grow myself.
  • To understand the stages of motivation and the stages of success.
  • To put together a process for building a product and for starting a business.
  • To face my fears and develop myself.
  • To build a site.
  • To put myself in a position where I must.

What do I need?

  • Motivation
  • Story
  • Research
  • Product
  • Marketing

The mindset to keep going​

The questions above are the easy part; following through is harder. This is the material I come back to when I need to keep going, kept as prose because it is meant to be read and felt, not answered.

The questions above are the easy part. Following through is harder. Here is the material I come back to when I need to keep going.

It's possible​

Your dream is possible. Others have done it, so why can't you?

You can live your dream. Choose your future, and live it.

It's necessary​

Improvement is not optional. Work on yourself, develop yourself, and keep asking: how can I improve this? How can I improve the lives of those around me?

Remove the energy drainers. People who stopped working on themselves, who stopped dreaming, will pull you down. Get the losers out of your life. Then align yourself with people who attract you upward: people who are unstoppable, unreasonable, unwilling to live life just as it is, and who want more.

Know this:

  • People like to complain, but they won't do anything about their situation.
  • You are an uncommon breed. Not everybody will see it, join you, or share the vision, and that is fine.
  • I can do this. Even though people don't see it for me, I can see it for myself.

Understand the importance of adjusting your strategy. Become creative. Be flexible. Be a good model of truth, integrity, and commitment. Surround yourself with winners.

If not you, then who? If not now, then when?

You are the author of your own story. Setbacks are part of the path. Review your dream every day, and remember: it's not over until I win.

It's me​

No one can do it but you.

  • Have goals, make a plan, and think constantly about how you can improve.
  • Take personal responsibility. Make it your personal business to succeed, and to come through when everyone is doubting you.

Face your fears​

Run toward your dream. Work toward it. Act on your ideas. Life is about stretching, challenging yourself, and working on ways to improve.

In the process you will incur disappointment, failure, pain, setbacks, and defeats. But you will also discover that you have greatness within you, that you are more powerful than you can begin to imagine, that you are greater than your circumstances, and that you don't have to go through life as the victim.

Most people don't work on their dreams. The reason is usually fear: fear of failure, fear of success. Face your fears. Put yourself in a position where you must.

And when you don't feel worthy, take risks on yourself anyway. Most people are not risk takers. They earn a living, raise a family, and die. Believe in yourself.

Someone's opinion of you does not have to become your reality.

item.title: Questions for Starting a Blog
item.description: Five steps of questions to work through before starting a blog: why, name, content, technology, and how you'll market it.
item.content:encoded:
Before you spin up a blog, it helps to settle a handful of questions first. Here is a five-step set to work through, from why you're doing it to how you'll get it in front of people. Answer these and the rest gets a lot easier.

Step 1: Why?​

Every later decision (the name, the cadence, the tech, the marketing) inherits its answer from this one. A blog without a clear why drifts and dies, so it is worth getting honest about purpose before writing a single post.

Step 2: The name​

The name is the first impression and the hardest thing to change later. A good one captures the vision from Step 1 in a few words, so it is worth pausing on rather than grabbing the first available domain.

Step 3: What content, and how often?​

Consistency is what separates a living blog from an abandoned one. Deciding the cadence up front (and what triggers a new post) turns writing from a someday intention into a commitment you can actually keep.

Step 4: What technology?​

The tech stack decides how much you will fight your tools instead of writing. These choices (hosted versus self-run, your own UI and backend, what you embed) trade convenience against control, and the right answer depends entirely on your why.

Step 5: How will you market it?​

Writing into the void is discouraging; even a little distribution changes everything. Deciding how people will find your blog (your resume, your friends, catchy titles, answering questions people are already asking) turns a private journal into something that reaches readers.

item.title: The Questions I Want to Answer
item.description: A running catalog of the big questions I find worth answering, about value, opportunity, society, and the act of questioning itself.
item.content:encoded:
This is an early, evolving catalog of the questions I find worth answering. It is more raw and aphoristic than my later question sets, and that is the point: it is a place to gather what I am curious about, figure out where I need to understand more, and consolidate the questions worth pursuing as part of a larger quest. Treat it as a living list, not a finished argument.

Value​

Value is where attention and money actually flow, and following that flow is the surest way to find what people genuinely care about. If I can read where value pools, I can read what to build.

Avoiding Failure​

Knowing why people fail is cheaper than learning it firsthand. If failure usually traces back to a missing resource, then resourcefulness becomes the thing worth cultivating.

Measurement​

What I measure is what I steer by, so the act of choosing the right questions to track is itself a high-leverage decision. These are the questions about which questions to keep close.

Resourcefulness​

Resourcefulness is the antidote to the failure mode above. Knowing what I have and what makes each resource special is how I do more with less.

Opportunity​

Opportunity often hides in arbitrage: a gap in knowledge or time that I can exploit before others do. Learning to spot those gaps is most of the game.

Purpose​

Beneath all the measurement questions sits the simplest one: what do I actually want to do? The idea that will not let go of me is usually pointing at the answer.

Motivation​

Motivation is the why behind the organizing. If I cannot explain why I have chosen to do what I am doing, the effort will not survive the hard stretches.

Metrics​

If answering questions is the work, then counting answers is how I know the work is happening. A simple tally keeps the practice accountable.

Learnings​

The questions I enjoy answering point toward the questions worth building a life around. Pleasure in the work is a reliable compass.

Spending​

How people spend money and time is the most honest record of what they value. If I can read that record, even from something as ordinary as the house someone wants, I can understand people better.

Data​

Every question above eventually needs evidence, and evidence needs a source. Knowing where the data lives is the difference between a hunch and an answer.

Resources​

Understanding what companies possess is how I learn to think about leverage at scale. Their resources are the raw material of everything they produce.

Planning​

Planning is one of the deepest questions in this catalog: who mastered it, whether it even matters more than direction, and how granular it should be. I want to understand the craft of planning at every scale, from a person to a country.

Thinking​

Automating thought is the frontier I keep circling: thinking, creativity, ideation. The deepest version of the question is not whether we can, but whether we should, and why we want to.

Growing​

Growth is so assumed that we rarely ask what happens when it stops, for a person or a company. Sitting with the absence of growth clarifies why it matters.

Purpose​

I keep returning to products because they sit at the meeting point of what I want to make and what people want. Understanding how good products get created is part of understanding what I want to do. I like products because they interface with people's carnal desires.

Society​

I want to better understand people: detecting the problems they have, and knowing how they respond. The goal is to lay a foundation for helping people understand people, with technology as the lever. Who has spent the time to model people's interests?

Questions on Questions​

This catalog is itself a system, and a system needs maintenance. These are the meta-questions about how to organize questions, whether they inherit from each other, and whether the set paints a complete picture without repeating itself.

Inference​

Inference is where credibility lives. I want to know how collective inference works, how accurate ours is, and where I want my word to be trusted, because the questions I dedicate my life to answering will only matter if people believe the answers.

item.title: What I Ask Myself: To Capture What I Could Do
item.description: How I brainstorm and organize everything I could do: goals, commitments, challenges, and the things I want to create.
item.content:encoded:
Before I can decide what to work on now, I have to see the whole field of what I could do. Prioritizing too early quietly throws away options I never even named. These questions are for the capture step: brainstorming and organizing my goals, commitments, challenges, and the things I want to build across every domain of my life, before any of it gets ranked.

What do you want to do?​

The first job of capture is to empty my head onto the page. Anything that stays unnamed can't be chosen later, so this section casts the widest possible net before any sorting begins. I record goals in the context of my roles.

Goals by category​

Capturing goals one domain at a time keeps any area of life from being silently skipped. Walking the categories deliberately, from career to spiritual, makes the blind spots obvious.

Career​

Family​

Financial​

Growth​

Health​

Learning​

Spiritual​

Commitments​

Commitments are the goals I've already bound my time to, which makes them the truest signal of my real priorities. This section audits whether what I've committed to still matches what I care about.

Challenges​

I need to consistently create new challenges to keep myself engaged. This section captures the hard things I'm choosing on purpose, because comfortable goals rarely grow me.

Things to build (artifactual outcomes)​

Some goals produce a thing I can point at: an app, a site, a document. Capturing those artifacts separately keeps the tangible outputs from getting lost among abstract aims.

Themes​

Across all my initiatives, a few recurring themes tend to repeat. Spotting them tells me where my real interests cluster and where my ideas keep coming from.

Why nots​

The fastest way to find missed options is to ask why I'm not already doing something. This section deliberately hunts for the things I've overlooked rather than the ones already on the list.

Reflecting on your initiatives​

Once initiatives are captured, they deserve a second look: what goals hide inside them, why I want them, and how they're shifting over time. Capture without reflection just produces a longer list.

Identifying projects​

A project is a bundle of work with a definite outcome, and it's the unit I actually execute on. This section turns loose ideas and learning objectives into projects worth committing to. Innovating, for me, is taking an idea and merging it with what I'm passionate about until it becomes a project bundle I can work on.

item.title: What I Ask Myself: To Decide What to Learn
item.description: How I choose what to learn next, build a curriculum, and keep up with the learning commitments I make to myself.
item.content:encoded:
There is always more to learn than time to learn it. The hard part is not finding material, it is deciding what deserves my attention next and then actually committing to it. These questions walk from picking topics, to prioritizing them, to building a curriculum, to protecting the time and keeping the commitments.

What to learn​

The first step is surfacing everything I might want to learn before judging any of it. Casting a wide net here keeps me from defaulting to whatever's trendy or in front of me.

Learning priorities​

With the field surfaced, the next job is ordering it. Difficulty estimates and value tell me what to learn first, so I'm not spending my best energy on the least useful thing.

Your learning plan and curriculum​

A plan turns intentions into a path: what I'll learn, by when, and in what order. This section builds the curriculum and the backlog so learning has structure instead of being a pile of bookmarks.

Making time for learning​

A plan is worthless without protected time, and learning time is the first thing the day eats. This section is about carving out and reclaiming hours, including the idle ones.

Learning commitments​

A commitment is what turns a plan into something I'm accountable to. This section pins down exactly what I've signed up to learn, how much time per week, and whether I'm keeping up.

Overlapping learning with work​

The cheapest learning is the kind that overlaps with what I'm already building. This section looks for where work and study can reinforce each other instead of competing for hours.

Consolidating your learning material​

Learning material scattered across bookmarks, links, photos, and screenshots is material I'll never actually use. This section is about gathering it all into one place I can return to.

Feeding your curiosity​

Underneath all the planning is curiosity, the engine that makes learning sustainable. This section tunes into what I'm actually drawn to right now, because forced learning rarely lasts.

item.title: What I Ask Myself: To Find Where to Improve
item.description: How I spot my gaps and weaknesses, find growth openings, and decide what to work on improving next.
item.content:encoded:
Growth starts with an honest map of where I stand. Before I can pick what to work on, I have to name the gaps, notice the openings, and decide what change I actually want. These are the questions I return to when I want to figure out where to improve next.

How to use these​

These work best in rough order: first surface gaps and weaknesses, then spot growth opportunities, then prioritize what's most valuable and aligned with my goals, and finally plan how I'll actually improve. The sections below mirror those steps.

Identifying gaps and weaknesses​

I can't fix what I won't name. This section is the honest inventory of where I'm weak, including a concrete checklist of specific areas to rate myself against so the gaps can't hide behind vagueness.

Growth opportunities​

Gaps tell me where I'm behind; opportunities tell me where I could stretch. This section looks forward, toward the new and uncomfortable, because growth lives where I'm willing to be a beginner.

Desiring change​

Improvement starts with actually wanting it. This short section checks whether I have a real desire to change, because effort without genuine want rarely sticks.

Learning from others​

Other people are a mirror for my own gaps. Spotting skills in them that I lack is one of the fastest ways to see what I should work on, and who I should learn it from.

Your improvement approach​

Knowing where to improve is useless without a plan to do it. This section turns the gaps into a concrete approach: the actions, the habits, and how it all ties back to my goals and learning.

Technical gaps​

Beyond general growth, there are specific technical things I want to understand better. This section names the concrete frameworks, tools, and processes on my list, like rasa, spaCy, Google Analytics, Spark, the Critical Path Method, and a Gantt-style view for ordering work.

Expanding your roles​

Growth isn't only about getting better at what I already do; it's about taking on new activities and roles. This section looks at widening the kinds of things I do, not just deepening them.

Enhancing your skills​

Some of the most valuable skills are the soft and structural ones I tend to neglect: persuasion, negotiation, showing my value, and modeling cleanly. This section targets those underinvested capabilities.

item.title: What I Ask Myself: About How I Learn
item.description: The questions I ask to understand and sharpen how I learn: my style, my process, and how I keep myself in motion.
item.content:encoded:
Most of us were taught what to learn, but rarely how we ourselves learn best. Getting deliberate about my own process changes everything: how I find a topic, how I absorb it, and how I know when I've actually got it. The questions below walk through learning style, process, seeking knowledge, expanding it, the methods worth studying, and how to keep the whole thing in motion.

Your learning style​

I learn differently than the next person, and naming how I actually take things in lets me lean into it instead of fighting it.

Your learning process​

A learning style is how I take things in; a process is the repeatable sequence I run so I'm always learning something on purpose. The bullets below are a starting shape for mine.

A starting shape for that process:

  • Capture a whole bunch of questions that intrigue me around a topic
  • Formulate a backlog to learn more about the topic
  • Come up with fun projects that may overlap with learning about one or more topics
  • As needed, come up with a plan for longer backlogs
  • Capture repetitive tasks

Seeking knowledge effectively​

A guiding principle for this section: employ the scientific method as much as possible. Treat curiosity as a hypothesis to test, not just a feeling to follow.

Expanding your knowledge​

Seeking knowledge gets me started; expanding it is the deliberate work of mapping, classifying, and connecting what I take in so it compounds. The list below is the sequence I run.

A sequence for expanding what I know:

  1. Map what I know and what I don't
  2. Identify all the areas of research that overlap with my interests
  3. Classify all the books I run into
  4. Figure out what I intend to read and for what purpose
  5. Identify an area in which I wish to grow and identify the books that will best help me achieve that goal
  6. Take notes while reading
  7. Expand the concepts I am aware of
  8. Track all the books I've read, and mark the pages that stand out

Learning methods​

There's more than one way to learn, and knowing the named methods lets me pick the right tool instead of always defaulting to 'read more.'

Topics worth learning more about:

  • Socratic Method of Learning (ask questions and find answers)
  • Instructional Design (crafting courseware to maximize how people learn)
  • Bloom's Taxonomy: kinesthetic (do), knowledge (know), affective (feel)
  • "Education is to understand complexity; training is to understand function"

Maintaining a learning mindset​

The hardest part isn't starting to learn, it's not stopping. These questions check whether I'm still a sponge or have quietly gone stale.

Self-teaching vs needing a teacher​

Some things I can figure out alone; others need a guide. Knowing which is which keeps me from either struggling needlessly or outsourcing what I should own.

Understanding knowledge​

Fields aren't islands. Seeing where the sciences intersect and where they share tools makes everything I learn reinforce everything else.

Capturing your learnings​

Knowledge I don't capture leaks away. These questions are about building the storehouse so what I learn stays findable and reusable.

item.title: What I Ask Myself: To Manage My Tasks
item.description: The questions I ask to capture, track, and automate my tasks: their dependencies, triggers, and the recurring ones.
item.content:encoded:
Tasks are the atoms of my work: the individual things that make up my plans and initiatives. But how I capture, track, and structure them determines whether my system actually serves me or just collects clutter. The questions below are prompts to interrogate my own task process: how tasks get in, how they move, what they depend on, and what could be automated.

Capturing tasks​

A task system is only as good as what makes it in. If capture is hard or unclear, half my work never reaches the list and lives in my head instead.

Tracking tasks​

Capture gets tasks in; tracking is how I know where each one stands and what to touch next. Without it, the list rots into a pile I avoid.

Immediate tasks​

Underneath tracking sits the now: the small set I can act on this instant. These keep me anchored to the next concrete action instead of the whole pile.

Desire to do​

Not everything I want to do has earned a task yet. Surfacing those wants keeps the wishlist honest and shows me when a desire has quietly died.

Capturing triggers​

Some tasks shouldn't live on a fixed date but should fire when something happens in the world. These questions are about catching those event-driven needs.

Recurring tasks​

A surprising share of my work repeats. Naming the recurring tasks (and dreaming up ways to regenerate them) keeps me from re-entering the same things forever.

Predicting potential tasks​

Beyond what already recurs, there's work I can see coming. These questions explore predicting tasks before they're due so they appear at the right moment on their own.

Task dependencies​

Tasks rarely stand alone. Many can't start until something else finishes or some event occurs, and modeling that is what keeps me from working things in the wrong order.

Automating tasks​

The least valuable work is the bookkeeping of tasks themselves. These questions hunt for what a script could create, recreate, or remind me of without my touch.

Breaking down work​

Some work is bigger than a task and deserves to be a story with a reason behind it. These questions are about decomposing effort and knowing why it's worth doing.

Structuring your tasks​

Tasks nest: subtasks roll up into tasks, tasks into projects, projects into goals. Seeing that hierarchy is what keeps a flat list from hiding the bigger picture.

Note: if weight is at least 1, the task is repetitive: it should happen around once a week.

The structure of my tasks is as follows:

Goal A
     Project A for Goal A
          Research A for Project A
          Task A for Project A
              Subtask A for Task A
         Subtask B for Project A
     Task B for Goal A
     Research B for Goal A
    Subtask C for Goal A

item.title: What I Ask Myself: To Plan Across Horizons
item.description: The questions I ask to turn my priorities into time-bound plans, from today and this week all the way out to a lifetime.
item.content:encoded:
Priorities only become real when they meet a calendar. Planning is how I translate "what matters" into time-bound action across horizons: today, this week, this quarter, this year, and the whole arc of a life. Use the questions below as prompts. Pick the horizon you're working in, and answer honestly rather than aspirationally.

Understanding time and planning​

Before I plan a single day, I need to understand what planning even is and how I actually spend my time, not how I imagine I do.

A few framing ideas worth holding onto:

  • Planning is part of time management; it is preparing for a future.
  • A plan is a sequential set of steps to help everyone reach a common goal.
  • If we don't all adhere to the same plan, we may step on each other's toes.
  • A plan considers priorities and chains them together.

Further reading: Time management vs. task management and Planning in time management.

Knowing why you're doing what you're doing​

A plan without a reason behind each item is just a schedule. I want to always be able to justify what I'm doing and what I'm choosing not to do.

I don't know when I'll be asked for a status update, but I need to be confident and clear, because I should always know what I am doing and why I am doing it.

Recurring activities​

Across all my horizons, the same handful of activities keep showing up. Naming them tells me what my time really goes toward, week after week.

Daily planning​

The shortest horizon. A day is where intention either turns into action or quietly evaporates, so I name what today is actually for.

Weekly planning​

A week is the unit where I can both refine the last stretch and set up the next. It's the rhythm where most of my real adjustments happen.

Quarterly planning​

A quarter is long enough to make real progress and short enough to course-correct. It's where weekly motion connects to yearly ambition.

Yearly planning​

A year is long enough to change who I am, not just what I finish. This horizon is where I name what I want to be different by next year.

Lifetime timeline​

The widest horizon. Zooming out to decades reveals the trajectory my daily choices are quietly drawing, and whether it points where I want to go.

Planning techniques​

Knowing the horizons isn't enough; I need techniques for replanning and for sizing a learning plan. These are the mechanics that make the horizons usable.

Reflecting on and assessing plans​

A plan I never check against reality is a wish. These questions close the loop: am I on track, and when do I adjust, re-prioritize, or change course entirely?

item.title: What I Ask Myself: To Prioritize My Work
item.description: The questions I ask to decide what to work on now: my prioritization factors, what to deprioritize, and how to build a roadmap.
item.content:encoded:
Capturing every idea is the easy part. The hard part is deciding what to do now from a pile of initiatives, projects, and things I want to learn. These are the questions I ask myself to focus, weigh trade-offs, deprioritize what no longer matters, and lay it all out on a roadmap. Read them slowly and answer the ones that snag on something.

Current focus​

Everything starts with 'now.' Before any framework, I name the single thing I most need to be working on at this moment.

How I prioritize​

Naming the now is easy; defending it is the work. This is the method underneath the choice: how I actually decide what's important and what wins.

Prioritization factors​

A good priority decision rests on factors I can name and weigh. These questions make those factors explicit so the call isn't pure gut feel.

Principles:

  • A task is higher in priority if it overlaps with many of my goals.
  • A task is higher in priority if it needs to be done immediately and is important.

By life category​

Priorities aren't only about work. Sweeping across career, growth, organization, learning, and family makes sure no whole area of life gets silently dropped.

Balancing priorities​

Two things constantly compete for my time: doing and learning. This question is about holding that balance instead of letting one starve the other.

What gets prioritized​

Before prioritizing, it helps to know what's even in the running and where it lives. This question is about taking inventory across roles and tools.

Prioritizing projects​

Projects are the big bets, so they deserve their own filter. These questions check a project against what I'll learn, enjoy, and can share before I commit.

I generally work on projects for one of two reasons: to learn something new, or to save myself time.

Aligning projects to objectives​

A project earns its place by advancing a real objective. These questions force each project to declare which goal it serves and what it'll teach me.

Projects reflecting priorities​

My projects should be a mirror of my priorities. When they aren't, that mismatch is the signal that I'm working on the wrong things.

Evaluating projects​

Once a project is in motion, it still needs a rubric. These questions are how I judge each project's benefit, alignment, and what it showcases about me.

Deprioritizing​

Prioritizing is also about subtraction. These questions are the hard ones: what do I stop, drop, or admit I no longer actually want?

Constructing a roadmap​

Priorities laid on a timeline become a roadmap. These questions place my goals in time and short-vs-long-term order so I can see the whole arc at once.

Tracking dependencies​

Goals lean on other goals. Knowing which depend on which, and what's in the way, keeps me from chasing a goal whose prerequisites aren't ready.

Retrospecting​

Prioritization is a skill, and skills improve by review. These questions close the loop: how well did my last set of choices actually serve me?

item.title: What I Ask Myself: To Align Action and Ambition
item.description: The questions I return to when I want to check whether my actions match my ambitions, and decide what to stop, start, or do more of.
item.content:encoded:
Ambitions are easy to declare and hard to live up to. The gap between the two shows up in what I actually do all day. These are the questions I use to close that gap: to notice where my actions drift from what I claim to want, and to decide what to stop, what to start, and what to double down on. I pick the ones that make me a little uncomfortable.

Action-ambition alignment​

My calendar tells the truth my intentions don't. These questions hold my daily actions up against my stated ambitions and name the gap, which is the whole reason the rest of the work exists.

What to stop doing​

Progress is often subtraction, not addition. The quiet 'why do I have to do this?' that surfaces when I'm resisting something is a useful signal that it may need to stop, so these questions hunt for what to cut.

What to start doing​

The most useful starting questions begin with 'why,' because they surface the thing I keep avoiding. These questions name what I've been putting off and turn it into something I'll actually begin.

What to increase doing​

Alignment isn't only about stopping and starting; it's also about feeding what's already working. These questions find the things I should simply do more of, especially the gift-developing ones that need more time than they get.

Challenging yourself​

Alignment without stretch turns into a comfortable rut. These questions check whether I'm actually at the edge of my comfort zone, and whether the pressure I'm putting on myself is the kind that forms a diamond or just wears me down.

Progress and purpose alignment​

It's possible to be busy, even challenged, and still drifting away from my purpose. These questions zoom out to ask whether all the motion is actually forward, and whether it's pointed at what I'm here to do.

Effort and values​

Alignment has two tests: am I giving it everything I've got, and does what I'm doing actually reflect what I believe? These questions check both my effort and my integrity at once.

Intentions​

Before action comes intention, and a muddy intention produces scattered action. Naming clearly what I intend to do is what gives everything downstream a direction to point at.

Taking action​

All the reflection only matters if it ends in a move. These questions close the loop: naming the concrete thing I'll do toward my goals and the plan that carries it into tomorrow.

item.title: What I Ask Myself: To Challenge My Own Assumptions
item.description: The questions I use to turn the lens on my own thinking, surface what I'm assuming, and steelman the side I disagree with.
item.content:encoded:
Most of the time we use questions to learn about the world. This set turns the lens around and points it at your own reasoning. The goal isn't to win an argument with yourself, it's to notice where you've stopped looking. Use these when you feel unusually certain, when you're defending a position hard, or when you suspect you're only collecting evidence that flatters what you already believe. Pick the ones that make you a little uncomfortable.

Identifying what to question​

Before you can challenge a belief you have to catch it in the act. These questions help me notice where my certainty is sitting so I know where to aim.

Challenging your thinking​

It is easy to collect evidence that flatters what I already believe. This cluster forces me to go looking for the parts that hurt.

Considering opposing views​

The fastest way to test a belief is to genuinely understand its opposite. These questions make me argue the other side as if I meant it.

Examining your reasoning​

These overlap on purpose: assumptions, evidence, and error are the three places reasoning quietly fails. Returning to them is a deliberate audit of the foundation.

Categories worth questioning​

When I can't tell what I've taken for granted, I run my thinking through these lenses one by one. Each one surfaces assumptions I'd otherwise never notice.

When you're not sure where to aim, run through these lenses and ask what you've taken for granted in each.

  • Setting: the story you're telling, the location, the interactions
  • Subjects: people and relationships
  • Role models and the abstractions you're reasoning from
  • Mood, emotions, celebration, kindness
  • Necessity and circumstances
  • Action, habits, time, buying habits, change
  • Improvement and growth
  • Concerns and worries
  • Usability, accessibility, affordability
  • Experience and memories
  • Assumptions, false assumptions, problems, issues, insecurities
  • Motivation and inspiration
  • Likes, desires, dislikes

item.title: What I Ask Myself: To Discover What Interests Me
item.description: I can't list my interests on command, so I infer them from what I do. These are the questions I use to reverse-engineer my own curiosity.
item.content:encoded:
Here's the trap with "what are you interested in?" you can't list your interests on command. You don't magically know them. The honest way in is to infer them from what you already do, read, build, and avoid. These are the questions I use to reverse-engineer my own curiosity: what draws me, what I'm done with, and what problems I keep circling back to.

Inferring interests from what you do​

I can't recite my interests on demand, but my behavior leaks them constantly. These questions read the evidence I'm already producing instead of waiting for a list to appear.

Topics and interests​

The obvious layer is still worth naming: what topics actually draw me, and which ones have quietly stopped. Tracking the change tells me where I'm heading.

Problems and questions​

My deepest interests tend to show up as problems I can't stop poking at. These questions look for the puzzles I'm drawn to solve, which is often the most durable signal.

Resources and tools​

What I want to read and which tools I reach for are quiet votes for my interests. These questions surface the resources my curiosity is already pointing at.

Projects and research​

Projects are where interests get tested against reality. These questions help me pick the ones that pull several threads together and actually move me toward my goals.

Identifying your passions​

Passions are interests that have hardened into something I'll return to without prompting. These questions help me tell a real passion apart from a passing enthusiasm.

Identifying your hatreds​

My aversions point at my interests from the other side. I hate wasting time on redundant work, which is probably exactly why I'm drawn to automation, so what I can't stand is worth reading just as carefully as what I love.

Activities and themes that recur​

When I look across what I keep doing, the themes repeat. I want to build, to identify and solve problems and puzzles. I'm passionate about composing meaning, which may be why I'm pulled toward both engineering and arts and crafts. I like designing things I think are valuable and seeing if they work. Part of my passion is pioneering, mixing and matching ideas, being creative. The recurring theme is structuring and organizing a thinking process so it can help others structure their own thoughts.

Problems worth dedicating yourself to​

Some problems are big enough to organize a life around. These questions stress-test a problem against whether it's truly mine and whether enough people share it to matter.

Narrowing down​

Eventually a wide field of interests has to collapse into a direction. These questions help me find the roles, works, and domains where my interests concentrate.

item.title: What I Ask Myself: When I Face My Fears
item.description: The questions I return to when I want to drag a fear into the open, see what's really threatening me, and find the courage to act anyway.
item.content:encoded:
Fear rarely announces itself honestly. It hides behind hesitation, busyness, and good excuses. These are the questions I return to when I want to drag a fear into the open and see it clearly, grouped by what they're really asking. Sit with the ones that make you flinch.

Core fears​

Most fear runs in the background, unnamed. These questions force me to say out loud what I'm actually afraid of, because a fear I can name is one I can finally examine.

What threatens you​

Once I know my fears, I look at what's actually behind them. These questions separate real threats from imagined ones so I can spend my courage where it counts.

Overcoming fear​

Naming fear is only half the work. This cluster is about the move from fear to action: what dissolves the fear, what I'd do without it, and how I pressure myself to grow through it.

Courage and faith​

Courage isn't the absence of fear, it's having something stronger to draw on. These questions find the source of my strength so I can reach for it when sight runs out.

Facing opposition​

Opposition, rejection, and setbacks are where fear becomes real and unavoidable. These questions prepare me to meet them on purpose instead of being caught off guard.

item.title: What I Ask Myself: To Find Purpose
item.description: The questions I return to when I want to understand why I exist, what gives life meaning, and how I want to be remembered.
item.content:encoded:
I don't think purpose is something you find once and file away. It's something you interrogate. Over the years I've collected the questions that actually move me when I sit with them, and grouped them by what they're really asking. This is that collection: a menu of prompts for reflecting on why you exist, what gives you meaning, and how you want to be remembered. Pick the ones that sting a little.

Core purpose​

These are the hardest questions to answer and the most important to keep asking. Most people live their whole lives without a clear answer, and that gap shows up as restlessness, drift, and doing things for the wrong reasons.

What gives you meaning​

Knowing what drives you is a navigational tool. When you can name your motivators specifically, you can design your life to include more of them, and notice faster when they're missing.

Why you do what you do​

Most people act on autopilot: their reasons are inherited, unconscious, or borrowed from someone else. These questions surface the actual drivers so you can decide whether to keep them.

Legacy and remembrance​

Legacy questions are useful not because you should live for posterity, but because they clarify what actually matters to you when you zoom out far enough to escape the noise of the immediate.

Vision and story​

You are writing a story whether you intend to or not. These questions ask you to become the author rather than just a character: to choose the arc, not just react to the events.

Mortality and finite time​

Mortality is the sharpest clarifier we have. Not to be morbid, but because the finite nature of time is what makes choices matter at all. These questions use that pressure productively.

Divine creation and becoming your best​

These questions assume that you were made with intention: that your capacity, your gifts, and your potential aren't accidents. Sitting with that assumption raises the stakes in a productive way.

Discovering your calling​

Calling isn't usually announced; it's discovered by noticing patterns. What you keep returning to, what you can't help caring about, what feels effortless to you and impossible to others: these are the signals. These questions help you read them.

Believing in purpose​

Purpose requires belief to become action. If you don't believe your life is for something, you won't do the hard work of finding out what. These questions check whether your beliefs are actually load-bearing.

Championing a cause​

A cause is bigger than a goal. It's what you're for in the world: what you'd put your name on, what you'd sacrifice for. These questions ask whether you've found yours.

Knowing you're getting closer​

Purpose isn't just about finding it; it's about tracking it. These questions build a feedback loop so you can tell when you're aligned and when you've drifted, rather than waiting for the feeling to hit you.

Avoiding regret​

Regret is information about what actually mattered to you. These questions use the threat of regret as a forward-looking tool, not to generate anxiety, but to clarify what deserves your effort now.

Listening to your inner voice​

Your inner life is constantly giving you information about whether you're aligned with your purpose. The problem isn't that the signal is absent; it's that most people have never learned to read it.

You possess an inner force trying to guide you to your full potential. It makes you feel unhappy when you stray from your purpose, and successful when you don't. Learning to recognize when you're near your purpose, and when you've drifted from it, is most of the work.

Understanding your uniqueness​

Your uniqueness isn't about being special; it's about being irreplaceable in a specific way. These questions help you identify the signature you leave on everything you touch, which is the practical definition of your calling.

The people who are closest to their nature will lead the world.

Purifying intentions​

Motivation matters even when the action is good. These questions check whether you're doing what you do for the right reasons, because the reason determines what the work costs you and what it actually produces.

item.title: What I Ask Myself: To Keep Growing
item.description: The questions I sit with when I want to know whether I'm truly improving, where I'm investing my energy, and if I'm living up to my potential.
item.content:encoded:
Growth is easy to assume and hard to verify. It's possible to stay busy for years without getting meaningfully better at anything. These are the questions I sit with when I want to know whether I'm truly improving, where I'm investing my energy, and whether I'm living up to what I'm capable of. Pick the ones that make you a little uncomfortable.

Growth mindset​

Growth starts as a posture before it shows up as progress. These questions check whether I actually want to get better, or just want to feel like I am.

Reaching your potential​

There's a gap between what I'm capable of and what I'm actually doing. These questions press on that gap, because unused potential is the quietest kind of regret.

Investing in yourself​

Growth requires deliberate inputs, not just effort. These questions audit what I'm actually putting into myself: knowledge, techniques, and the results I'm aiming at.

Self-knowledge for growth​

I can't grow well in a direction I don't understand about myself. These questions map how I'm wired so I can build on my real strengths and values.

Learning from masters​

I don't have to invent the path. These questions point me at the people who already walked it, so I can borrow their methods instead of rediscovering them slowly.

Measuring growth​

Growth I can't measure is growth I can't trust. These questions look for hard evidence that I'm improving rather than just the comfortable feeling of it.

The growth journey​

Two things keep me steady over the long haul: learning compounds like interest, so small consistent gains become large, and the visible competitions are the easy part while the real work happens behind the scenes. These questions keep me focused on that quiet, compounding work.

Acknowledging limits​

Honest growth starts with an honest map of what I don't know. These questions keep me humble about the edges of my knowledge so I don't build on ground that isn't there.

Key growth questions​

These are the questions I keep coming back to, the ones that most reliably move me forward. They turn the whole reflection into a concrete focus for what to learn next.

item.title: What I Ask Myself: To Operate Better
item.description: The questions I use to surface the invisible processes and systems I run on, so I can keep what works and retire what doesn't.
item.content:encoded:
Most reflection focuses on what you're doing and why. This set is about the how: the processes, techniques, and systems you operate by without ever naming them. The questions below are meant to surface those invisible methods so you can evaluate them, reuse what works, and retire what doesn't. Pick a section and sit with it.

Core processes​

The methods you run on are usually invisible until you name them. Putting language to how you actually operate is the first step to improving any of it.

Reusing and learning from the past​

Solutions you already found are wasted if you can't find them again. This section is about building a memory you can actually draw on.

Process evaluation​

A process is only worth keeping if it earns its place. These questions audit what you run on so you can add what's missing and drop what's dead weight.

Artifacts and constraints​

A good process leaves something behind and runs inside limits. Naming the outputs and the boundaries keeps your methods grounded in something real.

Automation​

Anything you do over and over by hand is a candidate for handing off to a system. This is where you reclaim time you didn't know you were spending.

item.title: What I Ask Myself: To Know What I Want
item.description: The questions I return to when I want to cut through the noise and name what I'm really after, what I'm chasing, and what I'd regret not pursuing.
item.content:encoded:
Knowing what I want sounds simple until I actually try to answer it. I chase plenty of things I never deliberately chose. These are the questions I return to when I want to cut through the noise and name what I'm really after, grouped by what they're getting at. I sit with the ones that make me uncomfortable.

Core desires​

Before I can chase anything well, I have to know what I actually want, and just as importantly what I don't. Naming both ends keeps me from spending a life pursuing things I never chose.

What you're chasing​

There's a difference between what I claim to want and what I'm actually running toward all day. These questions catch what's really pulling me, including the infatuations and turn-offs I don't usually admit.

Regrets and wishes​

Future regret is information about what actually matters to me, arriving early enough to do something about it. These questions borrow my deathbed perspective and bring it back to today.

Dreams and aspirations​

Dreams point further than goals, and they carry a feeling I want others to share. These questions ask not just where I want to end up but whether I'm actually living toward it or just longing for it.

Emotions and triggers​

My strongest emotional reactions are arrows pointing at what I care about. What moves me to tears or sends a chill through me reveals desires my rational answers tend to hide.

Mastery and likes​

Wanting to be good at something and being willing to pay for it are two different things. These questions separate what I'd like to master from what I'm actually ready to sacrifice for, and remind me to notice the small likes too.

Infatuation and bliss​

The clearest sign I'm pointed at the right thing is the feeling of being lost in it. These questions check whether what I do actually absorbs me, or whether I just think it should.

item.title: What I Ask Myself: To Know Who I Am
item.description: The questions I return to when I want to understand who I actually am: my character, my values, and the forces quietly shaping me.
item.content:encoded:
Why do others often know more about me than I know about myself? I rarely sit down and study the one subject I have the most data on. These are the questions I return to when I want to examine who I actually am: my character, my values, the beliefs and emotions quietly steering me, and the story I keep feeding myself.

Defining who you are​

If I can't name who I am in plain terms, every other answer rests on sand. Getting a working definition of myself is the foundation everything else builds on.

Examining your character​

Character is what's left when the performance stops, and the story I tell myself about myself quietly becomes that character. Watching the inner dialog is how I stay the author of it instead of the product of it.

Pay attention to your inner dialog. It's writing you whether you watch it or not.

Values and beliefs​

My values and beliefs are the code my decisions quietly run on. Making them explicit lets me update them on purpose instead of being governed by ones I inherited by accident.

What controls and shapes you​

I'm being steered by needs, fears, and constraints I didn't consciously choose, and some of them I've gotten too comfortable inside. Naming what controls me is the first step to deciding what I'll actually allow.

Becoming self-aware​

Self-awareness is the one study I keep postponing on the subject I know least. These questions push me to start the work and to define what 'aware' would even look like, so it's a destination and not a vague intention.

Capturing the essentials​

Sometimes I need a fast inventory of myself instead of a deep dive: what I think about, what I'm good at, what stops me, and how I get past it. This compact battery takes that snapshot quickly.

Learning about yourself through what's around you​

I'm not the only mirror I have. Watching society, nature, and the divine reflects parts of me back that I can't see from the inside, and that outside view often teaches me more about myself than introspection alone.

"I am" statements​

The way I finish 'I am' and 'I will' quietly declares my identity, and I can be guessed by it. Checking whether those statements line up with what I claim to be passionate about is how I tell who I really am from who I say I am.

Manifesting your beliefs​

Beliefs that stay inside my head don't shape anything. These questions push me to encode what I believe into something tangible, because the act of making values real is where identity stops being a feeling and starts being a contribution.
